text: Look, old man, you stay out of this. We spend a fortune on you, more than we can possibly afford, and all you do is criticize, morning to night. And then you think you can -

evidence: The speaker uses a dismissive address ('old man'), commands the listener to 'stay out of this', and accuses them of being a financial burden who does nothing but 'criticize' from morning to night.

text: No, it's right he should hear. I'm sick to death of it. We can't do anything right for you, can we?

1. ORSINI-ROSENBERG: Are you absolutely sure?

context_6_turns: 1. JOSEPH: Mozart, are you aware I have declared the French play of Figaro unsuitable for our theatre?

text: Well, Majesty, it is only a comedy.
en: Well, Majesty, it is only a comedy.

context_6_turns:
1. MOZART to JOSEPH: Yes, Sire.

6. JOSEPH to MOZART: Would you tell me why?
evidence: Mozart addresses Joseph as 'Sire' and 'Majesty', and Joseph asserts his superior rank by telling Mozart it is not his place to ask questions.

text: But, Your Majesty -
en: But, Your Majesty -
current_text: But, Your Majesty -
context_6_turns: 1. ORSINI-ROSENBERG: What you think, Mozart, is scarcely the point. It is what His Majesty thinks that counts.
evidence: Orsini-Rosenberg dismisses Mozart's opinion by asserting the hierarchy of the court and the ultimate authority of the Emperor.

text: Sire, I swear to Your Majesty, there's nothing like that in the story. I have taken out everything that could give offense. I hate politics.
en: Sire, I swear to Your Majesty, there's nothing like that in the story. I have taken out everything that could give offense. I hate politics.
current_text: Sire, I swear to Your Majesty, there's nothing like that in the story. I have taken out everything that could give offense. I hate politics.
context_6_turns: 1. JOSEPH: Mozart, I am a tolerant man. I do not censor things lightly. When I do, I have good reason. Figaro is a bad play. It stirs up hatred between the classes. In France it has caused nothing but bitterness. My own dear sister Antoinette writes me that she is beginning to be frightened of her own people. I do not wish to see the same fears starting here.
evidence: Mozart addresses the listener as 'Sire' and 'Your Majesty,' while the listener discusses his imperial power to censor Mozart's artistic work.

text: Ah, love again.
en: Ah, love again.
current_text: Ah, love again.
context_6_turns: 1. MOZART: But, Majesty, this is just a frolic. It's a piece about love.
evidence: Mozart addresses Joseph as 'Majesty,' establishing an imperial rank difference and a relationship based on court hierarchy.

text: Guess! Guess, Majesty. Imagine the longest time such a thing could last, then double it.
en: Guess! Guess, Majesty. Imagine the longest time such a thing could last, then double it.
current_text: Guess! Guess, Majesty. Imagine the longest time such a thing could last, then double it.
context_6_turns:
1. MOZART: But, Majesty, this is just a frolic. It's a piece about love.
2. JOSEPH to MOZART: Ah, love again.
3. MOZART to JOSEPH: But it's new, it's entirely new. It's so new, people will go mad for it. For example, I have a scene in the second act - it starts as a duet, just a man and wife quarreling. Suddenly the wife's scheming little maid comes in unexpectedly - a very funny situation. Duet turns into trio. Then the husband's equally screaming valet comes in. Trio turns into quartet. Then a stupid old gardener - quartet becomes quintet, and so on.  On and on, sextet, septet, octet! How long do you think I can sustain that?
4. JOSEPH to MOZART: I have no idea.
evidence: Mozart addresses Joseph as 'Majesty' and is pitching the technical merits and innovation of his opera, seeking the approval and engagement of his patron.

text: Well, I can speak for the Emperor. You made too many demands on the royal ear. The poor man can't concentrate for more than an hour and you gave him four.
